1) Mandate
- Find the current population of San Francisco as reported by three different authoritative sources (government, news outlet, and research institution). For each source, provide: the population figure, the date of the data, and a direct citation URL. Then identify discrepancies and state which figure is most likely accurate and why.

2) What I actually retrieved (summary of evidence available in this run)
- The search step located likely authoritative candidate URLs (for example, the U.S. Census QuickFacts page for San Francisco) but the subsequent visit steps in this run mistakenly fetched Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) pages instead of the San Francisco pages. How Census data is used
Learn how Census data helps community groups, businesses and governments make important decisions.
Shopping, fun and friendship: Census helps connect older people in Bass Valley
Beautiful beaches, a relaxed lifestyle and a strong sense of community are just some of the many reasons the residents of Bass Valley in rural Victoria choose to call it home.
More than a quarter of people who live in Bass Valley are aged over 65, compared to 15 per cent for the rest of Victoria.
Bass Valley Community Group Manager, Roderick McIvor, said Census data helps to inform services for the local community. “We look at Census data all the time because it helps us stay on course,” Mr McIvor said.
“Occasionally people might ask if we should be doing something else, like focusing on youth. From the Census, we know that older people form our largest demographic and really need our help.
“We can clearly see we have a large ageing population scattered across the Valley, including in some quite isolated locations. This isolation can put older people at risk of being lonely, and it can be hard to do simple things like shopping, social activities and meeting friends.
“We run a community bus to take passengers into town, out for lunch or to social activities like fishing and karaoke. The Community Group also runs events and clubs that give older people the chance to get together.”
By using Census data such as the socio-economic status, age and health profile of his community, Mr McIvor has been able to obtain grants to support his clients. Some of the grants have funded upgrading the op shop and starting a computer club.
“Census data allows us to understand what our community looks like, and working together we can determine where our volunteer services are needed most,” Mr McIvor said.
“The data gives us an insightful base to start our thinking. That’s why it's so important for people to fill out the Census. It’s essential to keep community services like ours running.”

result.content:
Population
Population clock and pyramid
Up to the minute projection of Australia’s population and population estimates back to 1981 and projections out to 2066.
Release date and time
18 December 2025
National, state and territory population
Statistics in this release are commonly known as Estimated Resident Population (ERP).
Australia’s population was 27,614,411 people at 30 June 2025.
The quarterly growth was 82,189 people (0.3%).
The annual growth was 420,100 people (1.5%).
Annual natural increase was 114,600 and net overseas migration was 305,600.
Reference period
June 2025
Release date and time
19 December 2025
Overseas Migration
Net overseas migration was 306,000 in 2024-25, down from 429,000 a year earlier.
Migrant arrivals decreased 14% to 568,000 from 661,000 arrivals a year earlier.
The largest group of migrant arrivals was temporary students with 157,000 people.
Migrant departures increased 13% to 263,000 from 232,000 departures a year earlier.
Reference period
2024-25 financial year
Release date and time
11 November 2025
Life expectancy
Life expectancy at birth was 81.1 years for males and 85.1 years for females in 2022
–
2024, reflecting no change for either males or females from the previous year (2021
–
2023).
Life expectancy was highest in the Australian Capital Territory for both males (82.0 years) and females (85.8 years).
Life expectancy was lowest in the Northern Territory for both males (77.0 years) and females (80.7 years).
Reference period
2022 - 2024
Release date and time
15 October 2025
Births, Australia
There were 292,318 registered births in 2024, an increase of 1.9% (or 5,320 births) from 2023.
The
total fertility rate
was 1.481 births per
woman
.
Median age was 32.1 years for mothers and 33.9 years for fathers.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
26 September 2025
Deaths, Australia
There were 187,268 registered deaths in 2024, an increase of 4,137 since 2023.
The
standardised death rate
decreased to 5.078 deaths, from 5.130 in 2023.
Infant
deaths increased by 46 deaths to 957.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
28 August 2025
Regional population by age and sex
The median age for capital cities (36.9 years) was younger than the rest of Australia (42.0).
The youngest capital was Darwin with a median age of 34.8 years, while Hobart was the oldest (39.3).
Darwin was the only capital with more males than females.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
30 April 2025
Australia's population by country of birth
At 30 June 2024, Australia’s population included 8.6 million people who were born overseas.
The proportion of Australia's population born outside Australia was 31.5%.
England, India, China and New Zealand were the countries of birth with the largest populations.
Those born in India recorded the largest increase since 2014.
Reference period
Jun 2024
Release date and time
27 March 2025
Regional population
The capitals grew by 427,800 people (2.4%) in 2023-24.
Capital city growth comprised overseas migration (373,000), natural increase (89,500) and internal migration (-34,600).
Melbourne had the largest increase (142,600), Perth had the highest growth rate (3.1%).
Regional Australia grew by 113,800 (1.3%).
Reference period
2023-24 financial year
Release date and time
16 July 2024
Historical population
Australia's population has increased from 3.8 million in 1901 to 25.7 million in 2021.
The urban population has increased from 58% of the total population in 1911 to 90% in 2021.
Fertility has decreased from 3.1 births per woman in 1921 to 1.7 in 2021.
Life expectancy at birth has increased by 26 years since the early 1900s.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2024
Household and Family Projections, Australia
The number of households is projected to increase from 10.0 million in 2021 to between 13.3 and 13.9 million in 2046.
The number of families is projected to increase from 7.2 million to between 9.5 and 9.7 million.
Living with a partner is projected to remain the most common living arrangement.
Reference period
2021 - 2046
Release date and time
03 April 2024
Population Projections, Australia
Australia's population in 2022 (26 million) is projected to reach between 34.3 and 45.9 million people by 2071.
The current ten year average annual growth rate (1.4%) is projected to decline to between 0.2% and 0.9%.
The median age (38.5 years) is projected to increase to between 43.8 and 47.6 years.
Reference period
2022 (base) - 2071
Release date and time
02 December 2022
Population: Census
The 2021 Census counted 25,422,788 people in Australia (excludes overseas visitors), an increase of 8.6 per cent since the 2016 Census.
49.3 per cent of the population were male with a median age of 37 years old.
50.7 per cent of the population were female with the median age of 39 years old.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2022
2021 Census overcount and undercount
The Census net undercount was 0.7% (190,044 persons).
The Northern Territory recorded the highest net undercount (6.0%) while the Australian Capital Territory recorded a net overcount (-0.6%).
Males were more likely to be missed in the Census (1.3% net undercount) compared with females (0.2%).
The net undercount for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people was 17.4%.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
03 August 2021
Regional internal migration estimates, provisional
104,100 people moved interstate in the three months to the end of March 2021.
16,400 more people moved interstate compared with the March 2020 quarter.
Capital cities had a net loss of 11,800 people from internal migration, the largest quarterly net loss on record.
Reference period
March 2021
Release date and time
17 June 2021
Migration, Australia
For the year ending 30 June 2020:
There were over 7.6 million migrants living in Australia
29.8% of Australia's population were born overseas
Australia's population increased by 194,400 people due to net overseas migration
368,700 people moved interstate, a decrease of 8.7% from the previous year.
Reference period
2019-20 financial year
Release date and time
14 December 2016
Census of Population and Housing Destination Zones
This product contains the digital boundaries for the Destination Zones (DZNs) used in the 2016 Census of Population and Housing
Reference period
August 2016

View article

View article

result.content:
Population
Population clock and pyramid
Up to the minute projection of Australia’s population and population estimates back to 1981 and projections out to 2066.
Release date and time
18 December 2025
National, state and territory population
Statistics in this release are commonly known as Estimated Resident Population (ERP).
Australia’s population was 27,614,411 people at 30 June 2025.
The quarterly growth was 82,189 people (0.3%).
The annual growth was 420,100 people (1.5%).
Annual natural increase was 114,600 and net overseas migration was 305,600.
Reference period
June 2025
Release date and time
19 December 2025
Overseas Migration
Net overseas migration was 306,000 in 2024-25, down from 429,000 a year earlier.
Migrant arrivals decreased 14% to 568,000 from 661,000 arrivals a year earlier.
The largest group of migrant arrivals was temporary students with 157,000 people.
Migrant departures increased 13% to 263,000 from 232,000 departures a year earlier.
Reference period
2024-25 financial year
Release date and time
11 November 2025
Life expectancy
Life expectancy at birth was 81.1 years for males and 85.1 years for females in 2022
–
2024, reflecting no change for either males or females from the previous year (2021
–
2023).
Life expectancy was highest in the Australian Capital Territory for both males (82.0 years) and females (85.8 years).
Life expectancy was lowest in the Northern Territory for both males (77.0 years) and females (80.7 years).
Reference period
2022 - 2024
Release date and time
15 October 2025
Births, Australia
There were 292,318 registered births in 2024, an increase of 1.9% (or 5,320 births) from 2023.
The
total fertility rate
was 1.481 births per
woman
.
Median age was 32.1 years for mothers and 33.9 years for fathers.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
26 September 2025
Deaths, Australia
There were 187,268 registered deaths in 2024, an increase of 4,137 since 2023.
The
standardised death rate
decreased to 5.078 deaths, from 5.130 in 2023.
Infant
deaths increased by 46 deaths to 957.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
28 August 2025
Regional population by age and sex
The median age for capital cities (36.9 years) was younger than the rest of Australia (42.0).
The youngest capital was Darwin with a median age of 34.8 years, while Hobart was the oldest (39.3).
Darwin was the only capital with more males than females.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
30 April 2025
Australia's population by country of birth
At 30 June 2024, Australia’s population included 8.6 million people who were born overseas.
The proportion of Australia's population born outside Australia was 31.5%.
England, India, China and New Zealand were the countries of birth with the largest populations.
Those born in India recorded the largest increase since 2014.
Reference period
Jun 2024
Release date and time
27 March 2025
Regional population
The capitals grew by 427,800 people (2.4%) in 2023-24.
Capital city growth comprised overseas migration (373,000), natural increase (89,500) and internal migration (-34,600).
Melbourne had the largest increase (142,600), Perth had the highest growth rate (3.1%).
Regional Australia grew by 113,800 (1.3%).
Reference period
2023-24 financial year
Release date and time
16 July 2024
Historical population
Australia's population has increased from 3.8 million in 1901 to 25.7 million in 2021.
The urban population has increased from 58% of the total population in 1911 to 90% in 2021.
Fertility has decreased from 3.1 births per woman in 1921 to 1.7 in 2021.
Life expectancy at birth has increased by 26 years since the early 1900s.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2024
Household and Family Projections, Australia
The number of households is projected to increase from 10.0 million in 2021 to between 13.3 and 13.9 million in 2046.
The number of families is projected to increase from 7.2 million to between 9.5 and 9.7 million.
Living with a partner is projected to remain the most common living arrangement.
Reference period
2021 - 2046
Release date and time
03 April 2024
Population Projections, Australia
Australia's population in 2022 (26 million) is projected to reach between 34.3 and 45.9 million people by 2071.
The current ten year average annual growth rate (1.4%) is projected to decline to between 0.2% and 0.9%.
The median age (38.5 years) is projected to increase to between 43.8 and 47.6 years.
Reference period
2022 (base) - 2071
Release date and time
02 December 2022
Population: Census
The 2021 Census counted 25,422,788 people in Australia (excludes overseas visitors), an increase of 8.6 per cent since the 2016 Census.
49.3 per cent of the population were male with a median age of 37 years old.
50.7 per cent of the population were female with the median age of 39 years old.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2022
2021 Census overcount and undercount
The Census net undercount was 0.7% (190,044 persons).
The Northern Territory recorded the highest net undercount (6.0%) while the Australian Capital Territory recorded a net overcount (-0.6%).
Males were more likely to be missed in the Census (1.3% net undercount) compared with females (0.2%).
The net undercount for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people was 17.4%.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
03 August 2021
Regional internal migration estimates, provisional
104,100 people moved interstate in the three months to the end of March 2021.
16,400 more people moved interstate compared with the March 2020 quarter.
Capital cities had a net loss of 11,800 people from internal migration, the largest quarterly net loss on record.
Reference period
March 2021
Release date and time
17 June 2021
Migration, Australia
For the year ending 30 June 2020:
There were over 7.6 million migrants living in Australia
29.8% of Australia's population were born overseas
Australia's population increased by 194,400 people due to net overseas migration
368,700 people moved interstate, a decrease of 8.7% from the previous year.
Reference period
2019-20 financial year
Release date and time
14 December 2016
Census of Population and Housing Destination Zones
This product contains the digital boundaries for the Destination Zones (DZNs) used in the 2016 Census of Population and Housing
Reference period
August 2016

c43b406e-c608-4f6f-855b-231cc4d977f6.title: Search for authoritative San Francisco population sources (government, major news, research institution)

details.query: San Francisco population 2023 2024 official Census QuickFacts San Francisco city California "population" "San Francisco" "QuickFacts" site:census.gov OR "San Francisco Chronicle" OR "Public Policy Institute of California" OR "PPIC" OR "UCSF" OR "research"
details.intent: Discover authoritative URLs for (1) government (Census/City), (2) a major news outlet article reporting a population figure for San Francisco, and (3) a research institution or think-tank report that states a population figure. Return top candidate links and snippets so subsequent visit steps can extract exact figures, dates, and citation URLs.
expected_output_fields: list_of_candidate_URLs_with_snippets (ideally including Census QuickFacts, a major news article, and a research/think-tank page)
details.why_this_reduces_uncertainty: Gathering candidate authoritative URLs first ensures the follow-up visits extract exact, citable figures rather than relying on memory or search snippets alone.
details.goal: Search for authoritative San Francisco population sources (government, major news, research institution)
details.original_goal: Search for authoritative San Francisco population sources (government, major news, research institution)
details.parent_goal: Find the current population of San Francisco as reported by three different authoritative sources (government, news outlet, and research institution). How Census data is used
Learn how Census data helps community groups, businesses and governments make important decisions.
Shopping, fun and friendship: Census helps connect older people in Bass Valley
Beautiful beaches, a relaxed lifestyle and a strong sense of community are just some of the many reasons the residents of Bass Valley in rural Victoria choose to call it home.
More than a quarter of people who live in Bass Valley are aged over 65, compared to 15 per cent for the rest of Victoria.
Bass Valley Community Group Manager, Roderick McIvor, said Census data helps to inform services for the local community. “We look at Census data all the time because it helps us stay on course,” Mr McIvor said.
“Occasionally people might ask if we should be doing something else, like focusing on youth. From the Census, we know that older people form our largest demographic and really need our help.
“We can clearly see we have a large ageing population scattered across the Valley, including in some quite isolated locations. This isolation can put older people at risk of being lonely, and it can be hard to do simple things like shopping, social activities and meeting friends.
“We run a community bus to take passengers into town, out for lunch or to social activities like fishing and karaoke. The Community Group also runs events and clubs that give older people the chance to get together.”
By using Census data such as the socio-economic status, age and health profile of his community, Mr McIvor has been able to obtain grants to support his clients. Some of the grants have funded upgrading the op shop and starting a computer club.
“Census data allows us to understand what our community looks like, and working together we can determine where our volunteer services are needed most,” Mr McIvor said.
“The data gives us an insightful base to start our thinking. That’s why it's so important for people to fill out the Census. It’s essential to keep community services like ours running.”

Population
Population clock and pyramid
Up to the minute projection of Australia’s population and population estimates back to 1981 and projections out to 2066.
Release date and time
18 December 2025
National, state and territory population
Statistics in this release are commonly known as Estimated Resident Population (ERP).
Australia’s population was 27,614,411 people at 30 June 2025.
The quarterly growth was 82,189 people (0.3%).
The annual growth was 420,100 people (1.5%).
Annual natural increase was 114,600 and net overseas migration was 305,600.
Reference period
June 2025
Release date and time
19 December 2025
Overseas Migration
Net overseas migration was 306,000 in 2024-25, down from 429,000 a year earlier.
Migrant arrivals decreased 14% to 568,000 from 661,000 arrivals a year earlier.
The largest group of migrant arrivals was temporary students with 157,000 people.
Migrant departures increased 13% to 263,000 from 232,000 departures a year earlier.
Reference period
2024-25 financial year
Release date and time
11 November 2025
Life expectancy
Life expectancy at birth was 81.1 years for males and 85.1 years for females in 2022
–
2024, reflecting no change for either males or females from the previous year (2021
–
2023).
Life expectancy was highest in the Australian Capital Territory for both males (82.0 years) and females (85.8 years).
Life expectancy was lowest in the Northern Territory for both males (77.0 years) and females (80.7 years).
Reference period
2022 - 2024
Release date and time
15 October 2025
Births, Australia
There were 292,318 registered births in 2024, an increase of 1.9% (or 5,320 births) from 2023.
The
total fertility rate
was 1.481 births per
woman
.
Median age was 32.1 years for mothers and 33.9 years for fathers.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
26 September 2025
Deaths, Australia
There were 187,268 registered deaths in 2024, an increase of 4,137 since 2023.
The
standardised death rate
decreased to 5.078 deaths, from 5.130 in 2023.
Infant
deaths increased by 46 deaths to 957.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
28 August 2025
Regional population by age and sex
The median age for capital cities (36.9 years) was younger than the rest of Australia (42.0).
The youngest capital was Darwin with a median age of 34.8 years, while Hobart was the oldest (39.3).
Darwin was the only capital with more males than females.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
30 April 2025
Australia's population by country of birth
At 30 June 2024, Australia’s population included 8.6 million people who were born overseas.
The proportion of Australia's population born outside Australia was 31.5%.
England, India, China and New Zealand were the countries of birth with the largest populations.
Those born in India recorded the largest increase since 2014.
Reference period
Jun 2024
Release date and time
27 March 2025
Regional population
The capitals grew by 427,800 people (2.4%) in 2023-24.
Capital city growth comprised overseas migration (373,000), natural increase (89,500) and internal migration (-34,600).
Melbourne had the largest increase (142,600), Perth had the highest growth rate (3.1%).
Regional Australia grew by 113,800 (1.3%).
Reference period
2023-24 financial year
Release date and time
16 July 2024
Historical population
Australia's population has increased from 3.8 million in 1901 to 25.7 million in 2021.
The urban population has increased from 58% of the total population in 1911 to 90% in 2021.
Fertility has decreased from 3.1 births per woman in 1921 to 1.7 in 2021.
Life expectancy at birth has increased by 26 years since the early 1900s.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2024
Household and Family Projections, Australia
The number of households is projected to increase from 10.0 million in 2021 to between 13.3 and 13.9 million in 2046.
The number of families is projected to increase from 7.2 million to between 9.5 and 9.7 million.
Living with a partner is projected to remain the most common living arrangement.
Reference period
2021 - 2046
Release date and time
03 April 2024
Population Projections, Australia
Australia's population in 2022 (26 million) is projected to reach between 34.3 and 45.9 million people by 2071.
The current ten year average annual growth rate (1.4%) is projected to decline to between 0.2% and 0.9%.
The median age (38.5 years) is projected to increase to between 43.8 and 47.6 years.
Reference period
2022 (base) - 2071
Release date and time
02 December 2022
Population: Census

f63ba2ce-7d71-49fd-a679-c92a3df102dd.title: Visit a research institution or think tank report that cites San Francisco population and extract figure, date, and URL

details.link_idea: A report or data page from a research institution or think tank (e.g., Public Policy Institute of California, university research center, or an academic data brief) that states a San Francisco population figure. Extract the population figure, the date of the data, and the direct citation URL, and note the data source they cite (e.g., Census, ACS, city data).
expected_output_fields: original_data_source_cited_by_report (e.g., ACS, Census estimates, city planning)
details.why_this_reduces_uncertainty: Research institutions often repackage or analyze official data; identifying the institution's figure and the primary data source they cite helps explain any differences from government or news figures.
details.goal: Visit a research institution or think tank report that cites San Francisco population and extract figure, date, and URL
details.original_goal: Visit a research institution or think tank report that cites San Francisco population and extract figure, date, and URL
details.parent_goal: Find the current population of San Francisco as reported by three different authoritative sources (government, news outlet, and research institution). For each source, provide: the population figure, the date of the data, and a direct citation URL. Then identify any discrepancies between the sources and explain which figure is most likely accurate and why.

How Census data is used
Learn how Census data helps community groups, businesses and governments make important decisions.
Shopping, fun and friendship: Census helps connect older people in Bass Valley
Beautiful beaches, a relaxed lifestyle and a strong sense of community are just some of the many reasons the residents of Bass Valley in rural Victoria choose to call it home.
More than a quarter of people who live in Bass Valley are aged over 65, compared to 15 per cent for the rest of Victoria.
Bass Valley Community Group Manager, Roderick McIvor, said Census data helps to inform services for the local community. “We look at Census data all the time because it helps us stay on course,” Mr McIvor said.
“Occasionally people might ask if we should be doing something else, like focusing on youth. From the Census, we know that older people form our largest demographic and really need our help.
“We can clearly see we have a large ageing population scattered across the Valley, including in some quite isolated locations. This isolation can put older people at risk of being lonely, and it can be hard to do simple things like shopping, social activities and meeting friends.
“We run a community bus to take passengers into town, out for lunch or to social activities like fishing and karaoke. The Community Group also runs events and clubs that give older people the chance to get together.”
By using Census data such as the socio-economic status, age and health profile of his community, Mr McIvor has been able to obtain grants to support his clients. Some of the grants have funded upgrading the op shop and starting a computer club.
“Census data allows us to understand what our community looks like, and working together we can determine where our volunteer services are needed most,” Mr McIvor said.
“The data gives us an insightful base to start our thinking. That’s why it's so important for people to fill out the Census. It’s essential to keep community services like ours running.”

How Census data is used
Learn how Census data helps community groups, businesses and governments make important decisions.
Shopping, fun and friendship: Census helps connect older people in Bass Valley
Beautiful beaches, a relaxed lifestyle and a strong sense of community are just some of the many reasons the residents of Bass Valley in rural Victoria choose to call it home.
More than a quarter of people who live in Bass Valley are aged over 65, compared to 15 per cent for the rest of Victoria.
Bass Valley Community Group Manager, Roderick McIvor, said Census data helps to inform services for the local community. “We look at Census data all the time because it helps us stay on course,” Mr McIvor said.
“Occasionally people might ask if we should be doing something else, like focusing on youth. From the Census, we know that older people form our largest demographic and really need our help.
“We can clearly see we have a large ageing population scattered across the Valley, including in some quite isolated locations. This isolation can put older people at risk of being lonely, and it can be hard to do simple things like shopping, social activities and meeting friends.
“We run a community bus to take passengers into town, out for lunch or to social activities like fishing and karaoke. The Community Group also runs events and clubs that give older people the chance to get together.”
By using Census data such as the socio-economic status, age and health profile of his community, Mr McIvor has been able to obtain grants to support his clients. Some of the grants have funded upgrading the op shop and starting a computer club.
“Census data allows us to understand what our community looks like, and working together we can determine where our volunteer services are needed most,” Mr McIvor said.
“The data gives us an insightful base to start our thinking. That’s why it's so important for people to fill out the Census. It’s essential to keep community services like ours running.”

Population
Population clock and pyramid
Up to the minute projection of Australia’s population and population estimates back to 1981 and projections out to 2066.
Release date and time
18 December 2025
National, state and territory population
Statistics in this release are commonly known as Estimated Resident Population (ERP).
Australia’s population was 27,614,411 people at 30 June 2025.
The quarterly growth was 82,189 people (0.3%).
The annual growth was 420,100 people (1.5%).
Annual natural increase was 114,600 and net overseas migration was 305,600.
Reference period
June 2025
Release date and time
19 December 2025
Overseas Migration
Net overseas migration was 306,000 in 2024-25, down from 429,000 a year earlier.
Migrant arrivals decreased 14% to 568,000 from 661,000 arrivals a year earlier.
The largest group of migrant arrivals was temporary students with 157,000 people.
Migrant departures increased 13% to 263,000 from 232,000 departures a year earlier.
Reference period
2024-25 financial year
Release date and time
11 November 2025
Life expectancy
Life expectancy at birth was 81.1 years for males and 85.1 years for females in 2022
–
2024, reflecting no change for either males or females from the previous year (2021
–
2023).
Life expectancy was highest in the Australian Capital Territory for both males (82.0 years) and females (85.8 years).
Life expectancy was lowest in the Northern Territory for both males (77.0 years) and females (80.7 years).
Reference period
2022 - 2024
Release date and time
15 October 2025
Births, Australia
There were 292,318 registered births in 2024, an increase of 1.9% (or 5,320 births) from 2023.
The
total fertility rate
was 1.481 births per
woman
.
Median age was 32.1 years for mothers and 33.9 years for fathers.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
26 September 2025
Deaths, Australia
There were 187,268 registered deaths in 2024, an increase of 4,137 since 2023.
The
standardised death rate
decreased to 5.078 deaths, from 5.130 in 2023.
Infant
deaths increased by 46 deaths to 957.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
28 August 2025
Regional population by age and sex
The median age for capital cities (36.9 years) was younger than the rest of Australia (42.0).
The youngest capital was Darwin with a median age of 34.8 years, while Hobart was the oldest (39.3).
Darwin was the only capital with more males than females.
Reference period
2024
Release date and time
30 April 2025
Australia's population by country of birth
At 30 June 2024, Australia’s population included 8.6 million people who were born overseas.
The proportion of Australia's population born outside Australia was 31.5%.
England, India, China and New Zealand were the countries of birth with the largest populations.
Those born in India recorded the largest increase since 2014.
Reference period
Jun 2024
Release date and time
27 March 2025
Regional population
The capitals grew by 427,800 people (2.4%) in 2023-24.
Capital city growth comprised overseas migration (373,000), natural increase (89,500) and internal migration (-34,600).
Melbourne had the largest increase (142,600), Perth had the highest growth rate (3.1%).
Regional Australia grew by 113,800 (1.3%).
Reference period
2023-24 financial year
Release date and time
16 July 2024
Historical population
Australia's population has increased from 3.8 million in 1901 to 25.7 million in 2021.
The urban population has increased from 58% of the total population in 1911 to 90% in 2021.
Fertility has decreased from 3.1 births per woman in 1921 to 1.7 in 2021.
Life expectancy at birth has increased by 26 years since the early 1900s.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2024
Household and Family Projections, Australia
The number of households is projected to increase from 10.0 million in 2021 to between 13.3 and 13.9 million in 2046.
The number of families is projected to increase from 7.2 million to between 9.5 and 9.7 million.
Living with a partner is projected to remain the most common living arrangement.
Reference period
2021 - 2046
Release date and time
03 April 2024
Population Projections, Australia
Australia's population in 2022 (26 million) is projected to reach between 34.3 and 45.9 million people by 2071.
The current ten year average annual growth rate (1.4%) is projected to decline to between 0.2% and 0.9%.
The median age (38.5 years) is projected to increase to between 43.8 and 47.6 years.
Reference period
2022 (base) - 2071
Release date and time
02 December 2022
Population: Census
The 2021 Census counted 25,422,788 people in Australia (excludes overseas visitors), an increase of 8.6 per cent since the 2016 Census.
49.3 per cent of the population were male with a median age of 37 years old.
50.7 per cent of the population were female with the median age of 39 years old.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2022
2021 Census overcount and undercount
The Census net undercount was 0.7% (190,044 persons).
The Northern Territory recorded the highest net undercount (6.0%) while the Australian Capital Territory recorded a net overcount (-0.6%).
Males were more likely to be missed in the Census (1.3% net undercount) compared with females (0.2%).
The net undercount for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people was 17.4%.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
03 August 2021
Regional internal migration estimates, provisional
104,100 people moved interstate in the three months to the end of March 2021.
16,400 more people moved interstate compared with the March 2020 quarter.
Capital cities had a net loss of 11,800 people from internal migration, the largest quarterly net loss on record.
Reference period
March 2021
Release date and time
17 June 2021
Migration, Australia
For the year ending 30 June 2020:
There were over 7.6 million migrants living in Australia
29.8% of Australia's population were born overseas
Australia's population increased by 194,400 people due to net overseas migration
368,700 people moved interstate, a decrease of 8.7% from the previous year.
Reference period
2019-20 financial year
Release date and time
14 December 2016
Census of Population and Housing Destination Zones
This product contains the digital boundaries for the Destination Zones (DZNs) used in the 2016 Census of Population and Housing
Reference period
August 2016

Capital city growth comprised overseas migration (373,000), natural increase (89,500) and internal migration (-34,600).
Melbourne had the largest increase (142,600), Perth had the highest growth rate (3.1%).
Regional Australia grew by 113,800 (1.3%).
Reference period
2023-24 financial year
Release date and time
16 July 2024
Historical population
Australia's population has increased from 3.8 million in 1901 to 25.7 million in 2021.
The urban population has increased from 58% of the total population in 1911 to 90% in 2021.
Fertility has decreased from 3.1 births per woman in 1921 to 1.7 in 2021.
Life expectancy at birth has increased by 26 years since the early 1900s.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2024
Household and Family Projections, Australia
The number of households is projected to increase from 10.0 million in 2021 to between 13.3 and 13.9 million in 2046.
The number of families is projected to increase from 7.2 million to between 9.5 and 9.7 million.
Living with a partner is projected to remain the most common living arrangement.
Reference period
2021 - 2046
Release date and time
03 April 2024
Population Projections, Australia
Australia's population in 2022 (26 million) is projected to reach between 34.3 and 45.9 million people by 2071.
The current ten year average annual growth rate (1.4%) is projected to decline to between 0.2% and 0.9%.
The median age (38.5 years) is projected to increase to between 43.8 and 47.6 years.
Reference period
2022 (base) - 2071
Release date and time
02 December 2022
Population: Census
The 2021 Census counted 25,422,788 people in Australia (excludes overseas visitors), an increase of 8.6 per cent since the 2016 Census.
49.3 per cent of the population were male with a median age of 37 years old.
50.7 per cent of the population were female with the median age of 39 years old.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
28 June 2022
2021 Census overcount and undercount
The Census net undercount was 0.7% (190,044 persons).
The Northern Territory recorded the highest net undercount (6.0%) while the Australian Capital Territory recorded a net overcount (-0.6%).
Males were more likely to be missed in the Census (1.3% net undercount) compared with females (0.2%).
The net undercount for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people was 17.4%.
Reference period
2021
Release date and time
03 August 2021
Regional internal migration estimates, provisional
104,100 people moved interstate in the three months to the end of March 2021.
16,400 more people moved interstate compared with the March 2020 quarter.
Capital cities had a net loss of 11,800 people from internal migration, the largest quarterly net loss on record.
Reference period
March 2021
Release date and time
17 June 2021
Migration, Australia
For the year ending 30 June 2020:
There were over 7.6 million migrants living in Australia
29.8% of Australia's population were born overseas
Australia's population increased by 194,400 people due to net overseas migration
368,700 people moved interstate, a decrease of 8.7% from the previous year.
Reference period
2019-20 financial year
Release date and time
14 December 2016

This product contains the digital boundaries for the Destination Zones (DZNs) used in the 2016 Census of Population and Housing

synthesized.summary:
The merged results do not meet the stated goal (San Francisco population from 3 authoritative sources). Only one relevant lead was found: U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts pages for San Francisco city, CA. However, the subsequent “visit” steps appear to have navigated to the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) population/census pages (Australia-wide figures), which are unrelated to San Francisco. No major news outlet source about San Francisco population was captured, and no research institution/think-tank report about San Francisco population was captured. Therefore, no San Francisco population figure/date/URL triad can be reliably provided from these results, and discrepancies between San Francisco sources cannot be evaluated.

Provenance note: The only San Francisco-relevant provenance in the merged results is the Census QuickFacts URLs returned by the search node; all visited-page extracts are from abs.gov.au and contain Australia population statistics (e.g., 25,422,788 in the 2021 Australian Census; 27,614,411 at 30 June 2025 ERP), not San Francisco.
key_findings: Government source lead identified (search results): U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts for “San Francisco city, California” is available at https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/sanfranciscocitycalifornia and a related table view at https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/CA,sanfranciscocitycalifornia/PST045222 (from node c43b406e...).

Attempted government-source extraction failed due to wrong site visited: the ‘visit’ node pulled Australian Bureau of Statistics ‘Population: Census’ (Australia) showing ‘The 2021 Census counted 25,422,788 people in Australia’ (https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/people/population/population-census/2021), which is not relevant to San Francisco (from node c0e3ea0...).

Attempted major-news extraction failed: the ‘news outlet’ visit also went to ABS ‘Population’ topic page, not a news article about San Francisco population (https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/people/population) (from node 8f3f9e6f...).

Attempted research-institution extraction failed: the ‘think tank’ visit again went to ABS ‘Population’ page, not a research institution report about San Francisco population (https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/people/population) (from node f63ba2ce...).

Because only one plausible San Francisco source link was surfaced (Census QuickFacts) and none were actually extracted, there is no basis in these results to compare discrepancies across government/news/research sources for San Francisco.
